Below is a solid MVP blueprint for a **team notes app**. I’ll assume a web app similar to Notion/Google Docs-lite: teams can create shared workspaces, write notes, organize them, search them, and manage permissions. ## Product: Team Notes App ### Core MVP Features 1. **Authentication** - Sign up / log in - Invite team members by email 2. **Workspaces** - Each team has one or more workspaces - Members belong to workspaces 3. **Notes** - Create, edit, delete notes - Rich text or Markdown editor - Auto-save - Note title and content - Last edited timestamp 4. **Folders / Collections** - Organize notes into folders - Optional nested folders later 5. **Permissions** - Owner - Admin - Editor - Viewer 6. **Collaboration** - Shared notes across team - Show who last edited a note - Optional real-time editing later 7. **Search** - Search by note title and content 8. **Comments** - Team members can comment on notes --- ## Suggested Tech Stack ### Fast MVP Stack - **Frontend:** Next.js + React + TypeScript - **Styling:** Tailwind CSS - **Backend:** Next.js API routes or server actions - **Database:** PostgreSQL - **Auth:** Supabase Auth or Auth.js - **Storage:** PostgreSQL for notes, optional object storage for attachments - **Realtime:** Supabase Realtime or WebSockets later If you want the fastest path, use: > **Next.js + Supabase + Tailwind** --- ## Main Screens ### 1. Login / Signup Users can create an account or log in. ### 2. Workspace Dashboard Shows: - Team name - Recent notes - Folders - Members - Create note button ### 3. Note Editor Includes: - Editable title - Markdown/rich-text editor - Auto-save status - Comments sidebar - Share/permissions button ### 4. Team Members Page Allows admins to: - Invite members - Remove members - Change roles ### 5. Search Page Search notes by keyword. --- ## Database Schema Here is a clean PostgreSQL schema for the MVP. ```sql create table profiles ( id uuid primary key references auth.users(id) on delete cascade, email text not null, full_name text, avatar_url text, created_at timestamp with time zone default now() ); create table workspaces ( id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), name text not null, created_by uuid references profiles(id) on delete set null, created_at timestamp with time zone default now() ); create type workspace_role as enum ('owner', 'admin', 'editor', 'viewer'); create table workspace_members ( id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), workspace_id uuid references workspaces(id) on delete cascade, user_id uuid references profiles(id) on delete cascade, role workspace_role not null default 'viewer', created_at timestamp with time zone default now(), unique(workspace_id, user_id) ); create table folders ( id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), workspace_id uuid references workspaces(id) on delete cascade, name text not null, parent_folder_id uuid references folders(id) on delete cascade, created_by uuid references profiles(id) on delete set null, created_at timestamp with time zone default now() ); create table notes ( id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), workspace_id uuid references workspaces(id) on delete cascade, folder_id uuid references folders(id) on delete set null, title text not null default 'Untitled', content text not null default '', created_by uuid references profiles(id) on delete set null, updated_by uuid references profiles(id) on delete set null, created_at timestamp with time zone default now(), updated_at timestamp with time zone default now() ); create table note_comments ( id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), note_id uuid references notes(id) on delete cascade, user_id uuid references profiles(id) on delete cascade, body text not null, created_at timestamp with time zone default now() ); create table workspace_invites ( id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), workspace_id uuid references workspaces(id) on delete cascade, email text not null, role workspace_role not null default 'editor', invited_by uuid references profiles(id) on delete set null, token text not null unique, accepted_at timestamp with time zone, created_at timestamp with time zone default now() ); ``` --- ## API Routes Example REST API design: ```txt POST /api/workspaces GET /api/workspaces GET /api/workspaces/:workspaceId PATCH /api/workspaces/:workspaceId DELETE /api/workspaces/:workspaceId GET /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/members POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/invites PATCH /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/members/:memberId DELETE /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/members/:memberId GET /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/notes POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/notes GET /api/notes/:noteId PATCH /api/notes/:noteId DELETE /api/notes/:noteId GET /api/notes/:noteId/comments POST /api/notes/:noteId/comments DELETE /api/comments/:commentId GET /api/search?workspaceId=...&q=... ``` --- ## Permission Rules | Action | Owner | Admin | Editor | Viewer | |---|---:|---:|---:|---:| | View notes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Create notes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | Edit notes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | Delete notes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | Invite members | Yes | Yes | No | No | | Change roles | Yes | Yes | No | No | | Delete workspace | Yes | No | No | No | --- ## Suggested App Structure ```txt app/ layout.tsx page.tsx login/ page.tsx signup/ page.tsx dashboard/ page.tsx workspaces/ [workspaceId]/ page.tsx notes/ [noteId]/ page.tsx members/ page.tsx components/ AppSidebar.tsx NoteEditor.tsx NoteList.tsx FolderTree.tsx MemberList.tsx InviteMemberDialog.tsx CommentSidebar.tsx SearchBar.tsx lib/ supabase.ts auth.ts permissions.ts notes.ts workspaces.ts types/ database.ts notes.ts ``` --- ## Example Note Editor Component ```tsx "use client"; import { useEffect, useState } from "react"; type NoteEditorProps = { note: { id: string; title: string; content: string; }; onSave: (data: { title: string; content: string }) => Promise; }; export function NoteEditor({ note, onSave }: NoteEditorProps) { const [title, setTitle] = useState(note.title); const [content, setContent] = useState(note.content); const [status, setStatus] = useState<"saved" | "saving" | "unsaved">("saved"); useEffect(() => { setStatus("unsaved"); const timeout = setTimeout(async () => { setStatus("saving"); await onSave({ title, content, }); setStatus("saved"); }, 800); return () => clearTimeout(timeout); }, [title, content, onSave]); return (

## Recommendation: **Postgres**

For a small SaaS app with 2 developers, low initial traffic, and uncertain future requirements, I would choose **Postgres** as the primary database.

It gives you the best balance of:

- Flexibility as requirements evolve
- Strong relational modeling
- Good support for complex queries and reporting
- Mature tooling and ecosystem
- Reasonable operational burden, especially with managed hosting
- Lower migration risk than starting with SQLite or DynamoDB

Use a managed Postgres service such as Supabase, Neon, Render, Railway, RDS, Crunchy Bridge, Fly Postgres, etc., rather than self-hosting at first.

---

# High-level comparison

| Database | Best fit | Main downside |
|---|---|---|
| **Postgres** | General-purpose SaaS apps with evolving requirements | More operational complexity than SQLite |
| **SQLite** | Very small apps, embedded apps, prototypes, local-first apps | Concurrency, scaling, and multi-instance deployment limitations |
| **DynamoDB** | High-scale, predictable-access-pattern apps on AWS | Harder data modeling, weaker ad hoc querying, higher lock-in |

---

# Postgres

## Strengths

Postgres is the safest default for a small SaaS app.

It supports:

- Relational data
- Foreign keys
- Transactions
- Indexes
- Full-text search
- JSON columns
- Background jobs / queues with extensions or patterns
- Analytics-style queries
- Row-level security, if needed
- Strong migrations and schema evolution

For a SaaS app, you will likely have entities like:

- Users
- Organizations / workspaces
- Subscriptions
- Roles / permissions
- Projects
- Settings
- Events
- Invoices
- Audit logs

These usually map well to a relational database.

## Tradeoffs

The main tradeoff is that Postgres is a real database server. You need to think about:

- Backups
- Connection pooling
- Migrations
- Indexing
- Query performance
- Version upgrades
- Monitoring

But with a managed provider, this burden is quite manageable.

## Migration risk

Postgres has the lowest strategic migration risk of the three.

If your app grows, Postgres can usually handle a long journey before you need to add anything else. You can scale it with:

- Better indexes
- Query optimization
- Read replicas
- Caching
- Partitioning
- Vertical scaling
- Background workers
- Separate analytics pipeline later

Also, if you eventually need DynamoDB, Elasticsearch, ClickHouse, Redis, or a data warehouse, Postgres can remain the system of record while those tools serve specialized needs.

---

# SQLite

## Strengths

SQLite is excellent for simplicity.

Its advantages:

- No database server
- Very easy local development
- Simple deployment
- Low operational burden
- Very fast for many small workloads
- Great for prototypes and embedded use cases

For a small internal tool or single-server app, SQLite can be a great choice.

## Tradeoffs

SQLite becomes trickier for a typical SaaS app when you need:

- Multiple app servers
- High write concurrency
- Managed backups and point-in-time recovery
- Read replicas
- Connection from many workers
- Separate background job infrastructure
- Easier observability
- More standard SaaS hosting patterns

SQLite can scale further than many people assume, but it imposes architectural constraints. In particular, it works best when one application instance owns the database file or when you use a specialized platform like Turso/libSQL.

## Migration risk

Migration risk from SQLite to Postgres is moderate.

The SQL dialects are similar but not identical. Differences can appear around:

- Types
- Date/time behavior
- JSON functions
- Constraints
- Concurrency assumptions
- Migrations
- Full-text search
- Upserts
- Locking behavior

If you know you may become a multi-tenant SaaS with multiple app instances, background workers, billing, audit logs, permissions, and reporting, starting on SQLite may save time early but create a migration project later.

## Operational burden

SQLite has the lowest operational burden at first.

But as soon as you need cloud-style deployment with multiple services, backups, monitoring, and failover, the burden can shift from “database ops” to “architecture workarounds.”

---

# DynamoDB

## Strengths

DynamoDB is excellent when you need:

- Massive scale
- Very high availability
- Low-latency key-value access
- Serverless AWS-native architecture
- Predictable access patterns
- Minimal database server management

It can be a great fit for event-heavy systems, IoT workloads, high-throughput APIs, shopping carts, session stores, and serverless apps.

## Tradeoffs

DynamoDB is not a general-purpose relational database.

The biggest issue is that you need to know your access patterns early. You usually design tables around queries, not entities. That can be difficult when product requirements are uncertain.

You give up or complicate:

- Joins
- Ad hoc querying
- Relational constraints
- Multi-table transactions in the traditional sense
- Flexible reporting
- Exploratory product analytics
- Simple admin dashboards
- Easy data correction and debugging

For a small SaaS team, DynamoDB can slow down development because application logic often becomes responsible for things that a relational database would handle naturally.

## Migration risk

DynamoDB has the highest migration risk if you choose it too early.

If your future requirements shift toward relational workflows, reporting, permissions, billing, complex filtering, or customer-specific admin queries, you may need to redesign your data model or migrate to a relational database.

Also, DynamoDB data models are often application-specific. That makes migrations more bespoke.

## Operational burden

DynamoDB has low infrastructure burden but higher design burden.

You do not manage servers, replication, or patching. However, you do need to manage:

- Partition key design
- Sort key design
- Secondary indexes
- Hot partitions
- Capacity mode and costs
- Query access patterns
- Denormalization
- Backfill workflows
- AWS lock-in

For two developers, that cognitive load may be more expensive than operating managed Postgres.

---

# Main tradeoffs

## Flexibility

Best to worst:

1. **Postgres**
2. **SQLite**
3. **DynamoDB**

Postgres is best when requirements are uncertain. You can model normalized data today, add JSON where useful, and query flexibly later.

DynamoDB is best when requirements are known and access patterns are stable.

SQLite is flexible locally but less flexible operationally as SaaS infrastructure grows.

---

## Operational burden

Lowest initial burden:

1. **SQLite**
2. **DynamoDB**
3. **Managed Postgres**
4. **Self-hosted Postgres**

But “operational burden” is not only server maintenance.

DynamoDB removes server ops but adds data modeling complexity. SQLite removes the server but can add deployment constraints. Managed Postgres has some operational overhead, but it is familiar, well-documented, and widely supported.

For a small SaaS, **managed Postgres is usually the best operational compromise**.

---

## Scaling

For low initial traffic, all three are fine.

Longer-term:

- **Postgres** scales very far for typical SaaS workloads.
- **SQLite** can scale surprisingly well, but mostly within certain deployment shapes.
- **DynamoDB** scales the most seamlessly for massive key-value workloads.

But scale should not dominate the initial decision unless you already know you need extreme throughput.

Most SaaS apps hit product, distribution, billing, onboarding, and retention problems long before Postgres scaling problems.

---

## Developer productivity

Best default:

1. **Postgres**
2. **SQLite**
3. **DynamoDB**

Postgres has excellent support across ORMs, migration tools, admin UIs, BI tools, and hosting providers.

SQLite is very productive early, but production SaaS patterns can become awkward.

DynamoDB can be productive for teams already deeply familiar with single-table design and AWS serverless patterns, but otherwise it has a steeper learning curve.

---

# Migration risk summary

## Starting with Postgres

Low migration risk.

You can likely stay on Postgres for years. If needed, you can add specialized systems later.

## Starting with SQLite

Moderate migration risk.

Good for prototyping, but you may eventually need to migrate to Postgres once you need multi-instance deployment, stronger concurrency, managed operations, or richer production tooling.

## Starting with DynamoDB

High migration risk if requirements are uncertain.

If your product evolves in a relational or analytics-heavy direction, the data model may fight you.

---

# What would make me change the recommendation?

## I would choose SQLite if:

- The app is very small and likely to stay that way
- You want the simplest possible deployment
- You are running a single server
- Write concurrency is low
- You do not need multiple app instances soon
- You are building a prototype, internal tool, or local-first product
- You are comfortable migrating to Postgres later if needed

Example: a small paid tool with a few hundred customers, low write volume, and a single-instance deployment.

---

## I would choose DynamoDB if:

- You are fully committed to AWS/serverless
- Your access patterns are known upfront
- You need very high scale or very low-latency key-value access
- You can tolerate denormalization
- You do not need many joins or ad hoc queries
- Your team is already experienced with DynamoDB modeling
- The app is event-driven or request-pattern-driven rather than relational

Example: a serverless API that stores user-specific state, events, sessions, or metadata with simple lookup patterns and very high expected traffic.

---

## I would still choose Postgres if:

- You have uncertain requirements
- You need standard SaaS entities like users, teams, roles, subscriptions, projects, and permissions
- You expect to build admin dashboards or reporting
- You want to move fast without over-designing access patterns
- You want a large ecosystem of tools
- You may need to hire more developers later
- You want to minimize future rework

---

# Practical recommendation

Start with **managed Postgres**.

Keep the setup simple:

- One primary Postgres database
- Automated backups
- Point-in-time recovery if available
- Basic monitoring
- Use migrations from day one
- Add indexes deliberately
- Avoid premature microservices
- Keep business-critical data relational
- Use JSONB only where flexibility is genuinely useful
- Add Redis, queues, search, analytics, or DynamoDB later only if a specific need appears

This gives you a stable foundation without overcommitting to either a single-file database architecture or a highly specialized NoSQL model.

For your situation — **2 developers, low initial traffic, uncertain future requirements** — **Postgres is the best default choice**.

**Recommend managed PostgreSQL.**

For a small SaaS with 2 developers, low traffic, and uncertain requirements, start with a managed Postgres instance (Neon, Supabase, RDS, Railway, Render, etc.). It maximizes developer productivity, query flexibility, and a clear growth path while keeping operational burden low.

### Why Postgres Fit This Case
- Two developers almost certainly already know SQL and relational modeling. Productivity matters more than theoretical max scale.
- Uncertain requirements favor flexible querying, joins, constraints, JSONB, partial indexes, full-text search, and ordinary migrations. You can evolve the schema iteratively without redesigning access patterns.
- Low initial traffic + managed offering = almost no ops work and negligible cost. Free/cheap tiers cover early stages. Connection pooling (PgBouncer or built-in) and serverless Postgres options handle the common web-app patterns.
- Multi-tenancy, transactions, auth-related integrity, reporting, and ad-hoc analysis are straightforward. SaaS apps almost always need these.
- Migration risk later is manageable: you can scale vertically, add replicas, use logical replication, partition, or move to specialized systems with proven patterns. Schema dumping and tooling (Prisma, Flyway, etc.) are mature.

### Main Tradeoffs
| Aspect              | Postgres (managed)                          | SQLite                                      | DynamoDB                                      |
|---------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Data model & queries** | Full relational + JSONB; flexible, powerful, standard SQL | Relational but single-file; full SQL for many cases | Key-value/document; access patterns first; no joins, limited queries |
| **Flexibility for change** | High – evolve columns/indexes/migrations easily | High for schema, low for concurrency/scale | Low – wrong key/GSI design is expensive to fix |
| **Ops burden (small team)** | Low with managed (backups, HA, scaling buttons) | None (embedded, just a file)                | Extremely low (serverless, fully managed)     |
| **Concurrency & multi-node** | Excellent (proper client pooling)           | Limited (single-writer; need special setups like Litestream/LiteFS/Turso for multi-instance) | Excellent (built for it)                      |
| **Scaling path**    | Vertical + replicas first; then more sophisticated if needed | Becomes painful for multi-instance SaaS; usually migrate | Infinite and automatic; overkill early        |
| **Cost at low traffic** | Cheap/free tiers common                     | Free                                        | Pay-per-request free tier often sufficient    |
| **Lock-in / ecosystem** | Low; portable SQL, huge ecosystem           | Low; but growth often forces a move         | High AWS; special data model                  |
| **Transactions/integrity** | Strong ACID                                 | Strong ACID (local)                         | Configurable (often eventual or single-item)  |

- **Postgres strengths for you**: developer familiarity, uncertain-reqs friendliness, enough headroom that you won’t hit walls for a long time, mature tooling for migrations/auth/search/etc.
- **SQLite strengths**: absolute simplicity, zero infrastructure, great for single-process or local-first prototypes, fast local development, trivial backups (copy the file).
- **DynamoDB strengths**: zero capacity planning, perfect for pure key lookups or very high/variable scale from day one, seamless integration if you are already deep in AWS.

### Operational Burden
- **Managed Postgres**: Near-zero day-to-day. You manage schema migrations, connection strings/pools, and (occasionally) index monitoring. Providers handle backups, patching, upgrades, and basic HA. For two people this is acceptable and usually less work than wrestling data-model changes later.
- **SQLite**: Lowest possible. Application code opens a file. Watch out for concurrent writes, proper file permissions/backups in containers/VMs, and how you run multiple app instances. Many teams eventually bolt on replication or migrate.
- **DynamoDB**: Lowest long-term ops once modeled correctly. Capacity modes, streams, and GSIs still require thought; modeling mistakes create operational pain (hot partitions, expensive scans, re-architecture).

For a two-dev team, prioritize low cognitive load and reversible decisions over zero ops if that zero comes with modeling constraints.

### Migration Risk
- **Starting with Postgres → elsewhere**: Medium and well-understood. Tools exist to dump/restore to other SQL databases. Moving to DynamoDB (or similar) later means rethinking access patterns entirely and usually dual-writing or batch ETL; possible but extra work. Risk is mostly “we might outgrow the convenience,” not “we painted ourselves into a corner.”
- **SQLite → Postgres**: Relatively low mechanically (compatible SQL dialects with small differences). Bigger risk is application architecture (connection handling, concurrency assumptions, multi-instance deployment). Many teams do this successfully once they need a real server.
- **DynamoDB → SQL or vice-versa**: Highest. Fundamentally different modeling (denormalized access-pattern driven vs flexible relational). You will rewrite significant data access code; reverse ETL and online dual-write periods are common. Early choice of DynamoDB with unclear access patterns is the highest-risk path for a small uncertain SaaS.
- Early on, staying relational (SQLite or Postgres) keeps options more open than jumping to DynamoDB.

### What Would Change the Recommendation
Switch (or start differently) if any of these become true:
- **Choose SQLite** if the entire app is intentionally single-process/single-machine (or simple multi-instance with a proven distribution layer like Turso/LibSQL, LiteFS), traffic stays tiny, you want absolute zero infrastructure forever, and you accept a future migration. Excellent for internal tools, local-first, or very early huge-bets prototypes.
- **Choose DynamoDB** if the access patterns are already crystal-clear and mostly key/single-item or simple queries, you expect sudden extreme/unpredictable scale or multi-region from day one, the other parts of the stack are already heavily AWS, and the team is comfortable modeling NoSQL (or prefers it). Also if absolute ops-free + infinite scale is the highest priority over flexibility.
- **Revisit Postgres** if you repeatedly hit write throughput limits after simple vertical/replica scaling, need extreme multi-region low-latency, purely event-driven key-value workloads appear, or total cost of ownership analysis shows DynamoDB or a specialized store is cheaper at your actual scale. Specialized needs (heavy graph, pure time-series, etc.) could also push elsewhere.
- Team skill or platform constraints matter: if both developers hate SQL or you are locked into edge/serverless platforms with first-class SQLite (or Dynamo) offerings and no good Postgres path, rebalance accordingly.

**Practical starting advice**: Use a managed Postgres with a modern migration tool + good ORM or query builder, add connection pooling early, keep the schema reasonably normalized but use JSONB where it helps, and instrument basic slow-query logging. This gives you speed of development now and optionality later. Only depart from it for a concrete, already-visible reason that outweighs the familiarity and flexibility advantages.

# Postgres, SQLite, and DynamoDB for a Small SaaS

## Recommendation: **Postgres** (managed, e.g., RDS/Supabase/Neon/Cloud SQL)

For a 2-person team with uncertain requirements, Postgres is the best balance of flexibility, ecosystem maturity, and low regret risk.

---

## Comparison

### SQLite
**Pros:**
- Zero operational overhead — no server, no network hop, trivial local dev
- Great for prototyping, tests, single-node apps
- Cheap (literally a file)

**Cons:**
- Weak concurrent write support — problematic once you have multiple app instances or background workers
- No built-in replication/multi-region story
- Painful to migrate away from later since your app logic and tooling assume "just a file"
- Limited ecosystem for things you'll likely want soon: full-text search, JSON querying, extensions, managed backups

**Verdict:** Great for MVP demos or truly single-user tools, risky if you expect any real multi-tenant SaaS growth.

---

### DynamoDB
**Pros:**
- Scales effectively infinitely with minimal ops once modeled correctly
- Pay-per-use pricing can be cheap at low traffic
- Fully managed, AWS-native

**Cons:**
- Requires upfront access-pattern modeling — you must know your queries before designing tables. This is the opposite of "uncertain future requirements"
- Weak for ad-hoc queries, joins, reporting, admin dashboards — you'll fight it constantly for anything not in your original access patterns
- Schema/index changes later (new GSIs, re-modeling) can require data migration and app rewrites
- Smaller pool of engineers who deeply know NoSQL modeling — costly for a 2-dev team to get right
- Vendor lock-in to AWS

**Verdict:** Excellent when you *already know* your access patterns and need massive scale — the opposite of your situation.

---

### Postgres
**Pros:**
- Flexible schema evolution — relational model handles unknown future requirements far better (ALTER TABLE, ad-hoc joins, migrations)
- Huge ecosystem: ORMs, admin tools, extensions (PostGIS, pgvector, full-text search), hosting options (RDS, Supabase, Neon, Fly)
- Can scale to a surprisingly large size (most SaaS apps never outgrow a single well-tuned Postgres instance)
- Easy for a small team to reason about — one mental model (SQL) instead of hand-rolled access patterns
- If you eventually need to shard, add read replicas, or migrate to something more specialized, you have a well-understood off-ramp

**Cons:**
- Requires *some* operational awareness (backups, connection pooling, migrations) — mitigated heavily by managed providers
- Not literally "serverless" scale-to-zero like DynamoDB (though Neon/Aurora Serverless narrow this gap)

---

## Migration Risk

| From → To | Risk | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| SQLite → Postgres | Medium | Doable but requires schema/query rewrites and downtime planning if done late |
| Postgres → DynamoDB | High | Requires full data-access redesign; not incremental |
| DynamoDB → Postgres | High | Undoing NoSQL modeling decisions is painful; often a rewrite |
| Postgres → sharded Postgres/Citus | Low-Medium | Incremental, well-trodden path |

Postgres has the lowest "regret cost" — you can start simple and grow into complexity (read replicas, partitioning, Citus, etc.) without a full rewrite.

---

## Operational Burden

- **SQLite:** Near zero, until it isn't (concurrency, backups, scaling)
- **Postgres (managed):** Low — provider handles backups/patching; you handle migrations and query optimization
- **DynamoDB:** Low day-to-day, but high *design-time* burden and ongoing burden if access patterns change

For 2 developers, minimizing *design-time* burden matters more than minimizing *server* burden — you don't have spare cycles to become NoSQL modeling experts.

---

## What Would Change the Recommendation

- **If access patterns are already fully known and simple** (e.g., single-table lookups, no reporting/joins needed) and you're committed to AWS → DynamoDB becomes reasonable.
- **If this is a single-user local tool or embedded app**, not multi-tenant SaaS → SQLite is fine, maybe ideal (e.g., libSQL/Turso if you want light replication).
- **If you anticipate extreme write throughput or global multi-region active-active from day one** → DynamoDB (or Cassandra-family) becomes more compelling despite the modeling cost.
- **If team gains strong NoSQL expertise** or hires a dedicated backend/infra engineer → DynamoDB's tradeoffs become more manageable.
- **If traffic is confirmed low and permanent** (e.g., internal tool, not really "SaaS" with growth ambitions) → SQLite's simplicity may outweigh Postgres's flexibility.

---

**Bottom line:** Postgres offers the best combination of low regret, flexibility for unknown future requirements, and a mature ecosystem that a lean team can lean on — without locking you into either SQLite's concurrency ceiling or DynamoDB's upfront modeling commitment.

## Findings

### High: `totalCents` can now return fractional cents
`totalCents` previously always returned an integer number of cents. With the coupon logic:

```ts
return subtotal - subtotal * couponPercent / 100;
```

the result can be fractional, e.g.:

```ts
totalCents([{ sku: "a", priceCents: 999, quantity: 1 }], 15)
// 849.15
```

For anything payment-related, fractional cents are usually invalid and can cause downstream rounding inconsistencies or API failures. The function name/type strongly implies integer cents.

**Recommendation:** Define and implement an explicit rounding policy, e.g. round down, round half up, round discount amount first, etc. For example:

```ts
const discount = Math.round(subtotal * couponPercent / 100);
return subtotal - discount;
```

But the exact rounding rule should be product/business-defined and tested.

---

### Medium: Coupon percent is not validated
Currently the function accepts any `number`, including negative values, values over `100`, `NaN`, and `Infinity`.

Examples:

```ts
totalCents(items, -10) // increases total
totalCents(items, 150) // negative total
totalCents(items, Infinity) // -Infinity
```

This is risky if coupon data comes from user input, config, or an external service.

**Recommendation:** Validate the coupon percent range and numeric validity. For example, reject non-finite values and enforce `0 <= couponPercent <= 100`, or document/clamp the behavior if that is intentional.

---

### Medium: `if (!couponPercent)` masks invalid values like `NaN`
This condition treats `undefined`, `0`, and `NaN` the same way by returning the subtotal.

`0` is fine, but `NaN` probably indicates bad data and should not silently skip the discount.

```ts
totalCents(items, NaN) // returns subtotal
```

**Recommendation:** Prefer an explicit absence check:

```ts
if (couponPercent === undefined) return subtotal;
```

Then validate `couponPercent` before applying it.

---

### Low: Return type is implicit and may hide the behavioral change
`totalCents` has no explicit return type. TypeScript infers `number`, but the semantic expectation is likely “integer cents.” Adding a coupon changed that behavior without making it obvious at the type/signature level.

**Recommendation:** Add an explicit return type:

```ts
export function totalCents(items: CartItem[], couponPercent?: number): number
```

And ensure tests enforce integer output.

---

## Missing tests

Please add tests for:

1. No coupon preserves existing behavior.
   ```ts
   totalCents(items) === previous subtotal
   ```

2. `couponPercent = 0` returns subtotal.

3. `couponPercent = 100` returns `0`.

4. Fractional discount cases, with expected rounding behavior.
   ```ts
   999 cents with 15% coupon
   ```

5. Invalid coupon values:
   - negative percent
   - over 100
   - `NaN`
   - `Infinity`

6. Empty cart with and without coupon.

7. Multiple items/quantities to ensure discount applies to full subtotal.

---

## Summary

The feature is straightforward, but before merging I’d want the coupon percent validation and cent-rounding behavior nailed down and covered by tests. The biggest correctness issue is returning fractional cents from a function whose contract appears to be integer cents.

## PR review: coupon discount on `totalCents`

### High

**1. Money can leave integer cents (correctness)**

`subtotal - subtotal * couponPercent / 100` does not keep results in whole cents:

```ts
// e.g. 101 cents, 33% off → 67.67
```

Previously `totalCents` effectively stayed integer when inputs were integers. Now it can return floats while still being named `*Cents`, which invites rounding bugs at payment/tax boundaries.

Prefer an explicit rounding policy (usually banker’s or half-up to nearest cent) and keep the return type integer cents, e.g. discount computed in integer arithmetic (or `Math.round` with a documented rule).

**2. Invalid coupon values are accepted**

```ts
if (!couponPercent) return subtotal;
return subtotal - subtotal * couponPercent / 100;
```

- `couponPercent > 100` → negative totals  
- `couponPercent < 0` → surcharge (silent)  
- `NaN` → treated as “no coupon” via falsy check (masks bad input)  
- Non-finite values / non-numbers at runtime likewise slip through

At minimum clamp or validate to `[0, 100]` (or throw on out-of-range) so totals stay non-negative and predictable.

### Medium

**3. Falsy guard is brittle**

`!couponPercent` conflates “missing”, `0`, and `NaN`.

`0` happening to mean “no discount” is fine, but prefer:

```ts
if (couponPercent == null) return subtotal;
```

…then validate the number. That makes “no coupon” distinct from “0% coupon” and avoids accidental `NaN` elision.

**4. API couples pricing concerns into cart total**

Discount application is inlined into `totalCents`. That will grow (fixed amount coupons, per-item rules, tax order, stacking). Maintainability is better if you split:

- pure subtotal  
- pure `applyPercentOff(subtotal, percent)` (with validation + rounding)

Even a private helper would help test discount math without cart fixtures.

**5. Semantics undocumented**

Callers must assume percent is `0–100` (not `0–1`). Name alone doesn’t lock that in; a short JSDoc (range, rounding, empty cart) would reduce misuse.

### Low

**6. No overflow / input hygiene on items**

Pre-existing, but more important once discounts ship: negative `quantity` / `priceCents` still produce nonsense totals. Worth a note or guard if this is a public util.

**7. Decimal drift**

Even before rounding, `*`/`/` on JS numbers is binary float. For money, integer math on cents (or a decimal library) is preferable long-term.

---

### Missing tests

No tests in the diff. Add at least:

| Case | Expectation |
|------|-------------|
| No coupon / `undefined` | Same as legacy subtotal |
| `couponPercent: 0` | Unchanged subtotal |
| Nice percent (e.g. 10% of 1000) | Exact integer result |
| Percent that doesn’t divide evenly | Documented rounded cents |
| `100` | `0` (if allowed) |
| `> 100`, `< 0`, `NaN` | Reject or clamp (per chosen policy) |
| Empty cart + coupon | `0` |
| Multiple line items | Discount on **subtotal**, not per line (confirm intentional) |
| Backward compat | Existing callers with one arg still pass |

Also assertion that return value is an integer if that’s the contract.

---

### Summary

The feature is a small optional parameter, so the change surface is fine, but **correct cents handling and coupon validation are not there yet**. I would not merge until: (1) integer-cent rounding is explicit, (2) percent range is enforced, (3) tests cover the table above. Optional refactor: extract discount application so `totalCents` stays a pure sum.

## Review Findings

**1. No input validation on `couponPercent` (High)**
- Negative values would *increase* the total instead of raising an error.
- Values > 100 produce a negative total.
- No check for `NaN` or non-finite values.
- Suggest clamping or throwing: `if (couponPercent < 0 || couponPercent > 100) throw new RangeError(...)`.

**2. Floating-point precision issues with cents (High)**
- `subtotal - subtotal * couponPercent / 100` can produce non-integer results (e.g., fractions of a cent) since JS floating point arithmetic isn't exact. Since this is money in cents, the result should likely be rounded (`Math.round`) to avoid downstream issues (e.g., `4.999999999999998` instead of `5`).
- Recommend: `Math.round(subtotal - (subtotal * couponPercent) / 100)`.

**3. `couponPercent === 0` is falsy but semantically valid input (Medium)**
- `!couponPercent` treats `0` the same as `undefined`, which happens to produce the same result here (subtotal), so it's not currently a bug, but it's fragile — if logic changes later, a legitimate "0% off" call could silently misbehave. Prefer `couponPercent == null` for clarity of intent.

**4. Missing tests (Medium)**
- No tests included in the diff at all. At minimum, need:
  - `totalCents` with no coupon (regression).
  - `totalCents` with a valid coupon (e.g., 10%, 50%, 100%).
  - `couponPercent = 0`.
  - Negative or >100 coupon (should throw or be clamped, once fixed).
  - Rounding edge case (e.g., odd cents with percentage that produces fractional cents).
  - Empty `items` array.

**5. Type signature doesn't express intent (Low)**
- `couponPercent?: number` allows any number, including invalid ranges. Consider a runtime guard as above, and document expected units (e.g., "0-100" vs "0-1 fraction") in a comment/JSDoc, since this is an easy source of caller confusion.

**6. Naming/documentation (Low)**
- Function `totalCents` previously had a single obvious purpose; now it silently supports discount logic. Consider a JSDoc comment describing the coupon behavior and rounding rules for future maintainers.

---

### Suggested fix
```ts
export function totalCents(items: CartItem[], couponPercent?: number) {
  const subtotal = items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.priceCents * item.quantity, 0);
  if (couponPercent == null) return subtotal;
  if (!Number.isFinite(couponPercent) || couponPercent < 0 || couponPercent > 100) {
    throw new RangeError(`couponPercent must be between 0 and 100, got ${couponPercent}`);
  }
  return Math.round(subtotal - (subtotal * couponPercent) / 100);
}
```

Overall: the change is small and directionally reasonable, but needs validation, rounding, and tests before merging.

Revenue is still rising, but only slightly: from **$82k to $97k**. Meanwhile, **churned customers nearly tripled** from **28 to 79**, and **support tickets also tripled** from **310 to 940**. What to investigate next:

1. **Ticket categories from March–June**
   - Are complaints concentrated around bugs, billing, product complexity, outages, missing features, or response times?

2. **Whether churned customers had recent support tickets**
   - If many churned customers contacted support shortly before leaving, the support experience or unresolved issues may be a churn driver.

3. **What changed around March/April**
   - Product release, pricing change, policy change, support staffing issue, marketing campaign attracting poor-fit customers, or operational disruption.

4. **Customer segment/cohort churn**
   - Are newer customers churning faster?
   - Is churn concentrated in a specific plan, industry, region, or acquisition channel?

**Rising churn and exploding support tickets (likely linked) are the core concern.**

Revenue and new-customer acquisition look healthy on the surface and keep growing, but the real risk sits in the sharp acceleration of churned customers (28 → 79) and support tickets (310 → 940). Net customer growth is already fading because the rise in churn is outpacing the rise in new customers; continued growth in this pattern will eventually stall or reverse revenue.

### Why this signal matters most
- Revenue growth is decelerating at the same time these two metrics worsen.
- Ticket volume is rising far faster than customer acquisition, indicating either product/process breakdowns, declining service capacity, or both.
- High friction and unresolved issues are classic leading indicators of the observed churn increase.

### What to investigate next
1. **Correlation and root causes of tickets → churn**: Segment the tickets by type, product area, customer cohort/tenure, and resolution time; check how many churned customers had open or escalated tickets in the prior 30–60 days.
2. **Recent changes**: Look for product releases, pricing/SLA shifts, support-staffing changes, or onboarding I process alterations that line up with the March–June acceleration.
3. **Operational capacity and quality**: Measure ticket backlog, first-response/resolution times, and CSAT/NPS trends; determine whether support headcount and tooling have kept pace with volume.

Fixing the sources of the ticket surge and the churn it is driving should be the immediate priority; without that, the revenue growth will not be sustainable.

## Key Signal: Churn is Accelerating While Revenue Growth Masks It

**The numbers that matter most:**

| Month | New Customers | Churned | Net Adds | Churn Rate (approx) | Support Tickets |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 140 | 28 | 112 | ~20% | 310 |
| Apr | 166 | 48 | 118 | ~29% | 520 |
| Jun | 172 | 79 | 93 | ~46% | 940 |

**Why this is the priority, not revenue:**

Revenue looks healthy (+18% over 6 months), but that's a lagging, easily-distorted metric — new signups and pricing can prop it up short-term. The real story is underneath:

- **Churn grew 182%** (28 → 79) while new customers grew only 23% (140 → 172).
- **Net customer adds are shrinking** (112 → 93) even though gross acquisition is still rising — meaning the business is now leaking customers faster than it can replace them, and revenue growth is likely being sustained by upsells/pricing on a shrinking base, not healthy growth.
- **Support tickets grew 203%** (310 → 940), almost perfectly tracking the churn curve — this is very likely the *cause*, not a coincidence.

**The pattern:** Support ticket volume and churn are rising in near-lockstep, both accelerating faster than customer growth. This strongly suggests a product/service quality or onboarding problem that started around March–April and is compounding.

## What to Investigate Next

1. **Ticket categorization**: Pull ticket data from March onward — are these bugs, onboarding confusion, billing issues, or performance complaints? Identify what changed around that time (a release, pricing change, new feature, capacity issue).
2. **Churn cohort analysis**: Are churned customers disproportionately *recent* signups (bad onboarding) or long-tenured customers (product regression/degraded service)?
3. **Ticket-to-churn correlation at the account level**: Do customers who churned have a history of unresolved or high-volume tickets before leaving? This would confirm causation, not just correlation.

**Bottom line:** Don't be reassured by revenue — the business is bleeding customers faster each month, and rising support volume points to a specific, fixable root cause. If this trend continues, churn will overtake new adds within 1-2 months and revenue will drop.

## Rubric for Evaluating Answers to the San Francisco 3-Day City Break Prompt

Score the answer on a **0–5 scale** for each category, or use the suggested point weighting for a 100-point rubric.

---

### 1. Correctness and Local Accuracy — 25 points

A strong answer should accurately reflect San Francisco geography, transit, pacing, and attractions.

**Excellent — 21–25**
- Correctly identifies relevant neighborhoods such as North Beach, Mission, Hayes Valley, Richmond, Haight-Ashbury, Castro, Ferry Building/Embarcadero, Japantown, Marina, or Noe Valley.
- Groups activities geographically to avoid inefficient backtracking.
- Recommends plausible restaurants, bookstores, scenic viewpoints, and nightlife options.
- Provides accurate transit guidance using Muni, BART, cable cars, ferries, walking, or rideshare where appropriate.
- Accounts for San Francisco’s hills, microclimates, and variable weather.

**Good — 16–20**
- Mostly accurate, with only minor issues in routing, timing, or neighborhood descriptions.
- Transit advice is generally useful but may lack detail.

**Fair — 10–15**
- Includes some accurate recommendations but has inefficient routing, questionable neighborhood pairings, or vague transit information.

**Poor — 0–9**
- Contains major factual errors, unrealistic geography, incorrect transit claims, or recommendations that do not fit San Francisco.

---

### 2. Completeness — 25 points

A strong answer should address every explicit requirement in the prompt.

**Excellent — 21–25**
- Provides a clear **3-day itinerary**.
- Includes **food**, **walking**, **bookstores**, **scenic views**, and **low-key nightlife**.
- Names specific **neighborhoods** for each day.
- Includes **daily pacing**, such as morning/afternoon/evening structure and built-in downtime.
- Provides **transit notes** for getting between areas.
- Includes **rainy-day alternatives** for each day or for major outdoor activities.
- Avoids an overly packed schedule.

**Good — 16–20**
- Covers most requested elements but may be light on one area, such as rainy-day alternatives or nightlife.

**Fair — 10–15**
- Gives a basic itinerary but misses multiple requested elements or lacks specificity.

**Poor — 0–9**
- Does not provide a coherent 3-day plan or omits most prompt requirements.

---

### 3. Fit to Traveler Preferences — 20 points

The answer should tailor the trip to someone who likes food, walking, bookstores, scenic views, and low-key nightlife.

**Excellent — 17–20**
- Food recommendations are thoughtful and varied, such as bakeries, casual restaurants, farmers markets, food halls, neighborhood dining, or iconic local options.
- Walking routes are scenic and realistic, not just transportation between locations.
- Bookstores are specifically included, such as City Lights, Green Apple Books, Dog Eared Books, Booksmith, or other appropriate options.
- Scenic views are well chosen, such as Coit Tower, Ina Coolbrith Park, Twin Peaks, Dolores Park, Lands End, Alamo Square, or the Embarcadero.
- Nightlife suggestions are relaxed and appropriate, such as wine bars, cozy cocktail bars, jazz, neighborhood pubs, bookstore events, or casual live music.
- The plan feels personalized rather than generic.

**Good — 13–16**
- Generally matches the traveler’s interests but may treat one or two interests superficially.

**Fair — 8–12**
- Includes some relevant activities but lacks consistent alignment with the traveler’s stated preferences.

**Poor — 0–7**
- Focuses on attractions or nightlife styles that do not fit the prompt, such as clubs, packed tourist circuits, or car-heavy sightseeing.

---

### 4. Pacing and Practical Usability — 15 points

The plan should be easy to follow and realistic for a relaxed city break.

**Excellent — 13–15**
- Schedule is not overly dense and includes natural breaks for coffee, meals, browsing, or rest.
- Daily activities are grouped logically by location.
- Provides clear sequencing without micromanaging every hour.
- Notes when to reserve restaurants, check opening hours, or allow flexibility.
- Gives practical walking/transit context, such as “walkable but hilly,” “take Muni back,” or “use rideshare if tired.”
- Offers useful tips for weather, layers, comfortable shoes, or timing popular spots.

**Good — 10–12**
- Mostly realistic, though may slightly overestimate walking capacity or omit some practical details.

**Fair — 6–9**
- Usable but somewhat rushed, vague, or inefficient.

**Poor — 0–5**
- Overpacked, confusing, unrealistic, or difficult to execute.

---

### 5. Assumptions and Transparency — 10 points

The answer should make reasonable assumptions and clarify them when needed.

**Excellent — 9–10**
- States or implies reasonable assumptions, such as the traveler staying centrally, not renting a car, preferring casual food, or being comfortable with moderate walking.
- Offers flexibility if assumptions differ.
- Avoids assuming a very high budget, extreme fitness level, specific dietary preferences, or late-night partying unless framed as optional.
- Notes that opening hours, restaurant availability, and weather may vary.

**Good — 7–8**
- Makes mostly reasonable assumptions but does not explicitly mention them.

**Fair — 4–6**
- Relies on unstated assumptions that may affect usability, such as long walks, expensive dining, or a specific hotel location.

**Poor — 0–3**
- Makes inappropriate or rigid assumptions, such as requiring a car, assuming nightlife means clubs, or ignoring mobility/weather constraints.

---

### 6. Risk, Safety, and Sensitivity — 5 points

The answer should avoid unsafe, insensitive, or misleading recommendations.

**Excellent — 5**
- Gives sensible safety and comfort advice without fearmongering.
- Avoids stigmatizing neighborhoods or unhoused people.
- Recommends reasonable caution with valuables, late-night transit, and weather.
- Does not encourage unsafe walking routes at night or trespassing for views.
- Avoids recommending permanently closed businesses or questionable activities.

**Good — 4**
- Generally safe and appropriate, with only minor omissions.

**Fair — 2–3**
- Lacks safety context or includes slightly impractical late-night/transit suggestions.

**Poor — 0–1**
- Includes risky, insensitive, or clearly unsafe advice.

---

## Overall Rating Guide

**90–100: Excellent**
- Fully satisfies the prompt, is locally accurate, relaxed, practical, and well tailored.

**75–89: Good**
- Strong and usable, with minor omissions or slight pacing/transit issues.

**60–74: Adequate**
- Covers the basics but lacks detail, customization, or polish.

**40–59: Weak**
- Partially answers the prompt but misses key requirements or is difficult to use.

**Below 40: Poor**
- Fails to provide a realistic, relevant, or complete San Francisco itinerary.

---

## Common Red Flags

- Packing each day with too many far-apart attractions.
- Listing tourist sights without neighborhoods, pacing, or transit notes.
- Ignoring bookstores or rainy-day alternatives.
- Treating “low-key nightlife” as clubs or bar-hopping.
- Suggesting a car as necessary for central San Francisco.
- Underestimating hills, fog, wind, or travel time.
- Failing to distinguish outdoor plans from rainy-day backup options.
- Providing generic recommendations that could apply to any city.

**Rubric for Evaluating 3-Day San Francisco City Break Itineraries**

This rubric assesses responses to the prompt:  

*Plan a 3-day city break to San Francisco for someone who likes food, walking, bookstores, scenic views, and low-key nightlife. Include neighborhoods, daily pacing, transit notes, and rainy-day alternatives. Avoid packing the schedule too tightly.*

**Scoring scale (apply to each criterion):**  
- **5 – Excellent**: Fully meets or exceeds expectations with high reliability and utility.  
- **4 – Good**: Strong, minor gaps or refinements needed.  
- **3 – Adequate**: Covers basics but has noticeable weaknesses.  
- **2 – Needs Improvement**: Partial, incomplete, or unreliable in key ways.  
- **1 – Poor**: Fails to address the criterion meaningfully or introduces major flaws.  

**Total**: Average the five criterion scores (or weight as preferred: Completeness & Usability slightly higher at ~20–25% each; others ~15–20%). Use qualitatively or convert to percentages. Flag any factual errors, safety oversights, or over-packing as automatic deductions regardless of other strengths.

### 1. Correctness (accuracy of facts, places, transit, and local fit)
Evaluates whether recommendations are factually right for San Francisco, match the stated interests, and use real, current (or clearly dated) information.

- **High (5)**: Accurate neighborhoods (e.g., Mission, North Beach, Hayes Valley, Richmond/Sunset or Embarcadero/Ferry Building areas for food + walking + views + bookstores); real bookstores, eateries (including low-key/appetizing options matching “food” interest), viewpoint walking routes (e.g., coastal paths, parks with skyline/ocean views), and low-key nightlife (wine bars, casual cocktail spots, bookstores with events, not clubs). Reliable transit (Muni, BART, cable cars/walks where appropriate) with realistic times/costs/notes. No invented places or outdated major closures. Aligns with SF’s topography, weather variability, and walkability without exaggeration.  
- **Mid (3)**: Mostly real places and transit, but minor inaccuracies (wrong hours, overstated walking ease between distant points, generic “hill” advice, or slightly mismatched vibe such as high-energy spots for “low-key”).  
- **Low (1–2)**: Fabricated or significantly wrong locations, impossible transit links, tourist-trap overload ignoring preferences, or claims that contradict well-known SF realities (hills, fog, public transit quirks).  

**Key check**: Cross-referenceable against current maps/transit apps and interest fit (walking + scenic + food + bookstores + low-key nights).

### 2. Completeness (coverage of all prompt requirements)
Evaluates whether every requested element is present and integrated for a coherent 3-day plan.

- **High (5)**: Full 3-day structure with neighborhoods named and why they suit the traveler; explicit daily pacing (morning/afternoon/evening flow that feels unhurried—“avoid packing too tightly”); transit notes (how to get between spots, tickets/apps, walking vs. ride options); rainy-day alternatives woven in or clearly listed for each day/key activities; balances all interests (food + walking routes + bookstores + scenic views + low-key nightlife) without omitting any; ends with practical wrap-up if helpful (packing notes light). Options exist for variation.  
- **Mid (3)**: Most elements present but thin on one or two (e.g., pacing implied rather than described, transit only named without notes, rainy alternatives generic or missing from one day, one interest underrepresented).  
- **Low (1–2)**: Missing major pieces (no rainy-day plans, no transit notes, only 1–2 days, schedule still feels packed, or core interests ignored).  

**Key check**: Does a reader leave knowing neighborhoods, how the days feel (pace), how to move around, and what to do if it rains—while seeing food/walking/bookstores/views/nightlife represented?

### 3. Assumptions (explicitness, reasonableness, and handling of traveler variability)
Evaluates what the plan assumes about the traveler, conditions, and logistics—and whether those assumptions are stated, justified, and flexible.

- **High (5)**: States reasonable assumptions clearly (e.g., moderate mobility for SF hills/walking, mid-range budget-flexible, English-speaking adult traveler without specific dietary needs or kids, late spring/fall weather window or adjustable, interest in local independent spots). Offers light alternatives or notes for common variations (dietary restrictions, fatigue, rain, arriving by different transit). Avoids unstated extremes (assumes unlimited energy, car ownership, or luxury only). Season, weather realism, and “low-key” interpretation are considered.  
- **Mid (3)**: Some assumptions implicit or lightly stated; a few reasonable but unaddressed (budget/mobility) that could break the plan.  
- **Low (1–2)**: Hidden or unreasonable assumptions (perfect weather only, extreme fitness, free time with zero recovery, high/low budget extremes not flagged, ignores accessibility entirely). No flexibility.  

**Key check**: Would different but still free-walking, food/bookstore-loving travelers still be able to adapt the plan safely?

### 4. Usability (clarity, actionability, structure, and practicality for real travelers)
Evaluates how easy the plan is to understand and execute without overloading the trip.

- **High (5)**: Clear day-by-day structure (time blocks or loose morning/midday/evening) with natural pacing, buffer time, and rest notes so it feels unhurried. Actionable details (neighborhood anchors, ~specific well-known or findable spots with “why,” approximate walk/transit times, rough order that respects geography). Easy to scan; language is practical and inviting. Avoids both sparsity and “dense checklist” feel. Includes just enough logistics to start (apps like Muni/BART, walking tips for hills/views) without essay length. Reader could follow or adapt it confidently on arrival.  
- **Mid (3)**: Mostly usable but uneven (some vague “explore” sections, occasional geographic jumps that feel tiring, or overly dense prose).  
- **Low (1–2)**: Confusing structure, packed or vague days, geography that forces stress, or so sparse it’s not actionable; reader can’t easily use it.  

**Key check**: Could someone with moderate travel experience implement it with a map app and still have a relaxed, enjoyable time matching the interests?

### 5. Risk (identification and mitigation of safety, logistical, health, weather, and experiential risks)
Evaluates awareness of downsides and how the plan reduces them while preserving enjoyment.

- **High (5)**: Notes key SF-real risks with mitigations: hills and walking fatigue (pace control, elevation hints, rest café options); variable weather/fog (layering + rainy alternatives already required); neighborhoods/time-of-day safety (stick to well-trafficked, well-lit low-key evenings; general awareness without fear-mongering); transit reliability (backup rideshares for hills/late); food (variety including walkable casual, basic allergy/accessibility nods if space); over-ambition (explicit buffers so it doesn’t become stressful). Avoids high-risk or mismatch activities. Encourages common-sense flexibility. Low-key nightlife kept safe/appropriate.  
- **Mid (3)**: Mentions weather and pace somewhat; other risks (hills, evening safety, transit quirks) under-addressed.  
- **Low (1–2)**: No risk awareness (aggressive schedule, outdoor-only rainy plans, isolated late-night spots without caveats, steep walking without notes) or creates unnecessary risk.  

**Key check**: Does the plan leave the traveler more prepared and safer/relaxed than if they had none—especially for walking-heavy, weather-variable SF?

### Additional scoring notes and usage guidance
- **Over-arching flags** (deduct 0.5–1+ full points or cap total): Schedule feels tightly packed despite claims; major factual errors; completely omits rainy-day plans or transit notes; ignores “low-key” or core interests; unsafe or inaccessible recommendations presented as default.  
- **Strengths that boost scores**: Local character (independent bookstores, neighborhood food culture, genuine view walks), graceful redundancy (multiple rainy indoor options like bookstores + cafés + markets), realistic energy curves, and geographic logic (clustering activities).  
- **How to apply**: Score independently then average/discuss. For multi-evaluator use, calibrate on one sample first. Prioritize Correctness + Completeness + Usability as core; Assumptions + Risk ensure real-world robustness. Best answers are specific yet flexible, accurate yet paced for enjoyment, and transparent about what they assume.  
- **Edge cases**: Purely hallucination-heavy or AI-generated generic “tourist SF” plans score low across Correctness/Usability/Risks even if they list days. Overly luxurious or budget-only plans require stated assumptions to score well.

This rubric produces consistent, fair evaluations focused on delivering a practical, interest-aligned, low-pressure San Francisco city break.

# Rubric: Evaluating a 3-Day San Francisco City Break Plan

## 1. Correctness (25 points)
Assesses factual accuracy about San Francisco specifically.

| Score | Criteria |
|---|---|
| 5 | Neighborhood names, geography, and adjacency are accurate (e.g., correctly places North Beach near Chinatown, not near Sunset) |
| 5 | Named venues (bookstores, restaurants, viewpoints) actually exist and are correctly located |
| 5 | Transit information is accurate (Muni, BART, cable car routes/limitations, walkability claims) |
| 5 | Distances/travel times between suggested stops are realistic (not suggesting a 20-min walk that's actually 2 miles uphill) |
| 5 | No fabricated details (invented restaurant names, wrong hours, nonexistent bus lines) |

**Deductions:** Any hallucinated venue or clearly wrong geographic claim should cap this section at 10/25.

---

## 2. Completeness (25 points)
Assesses whether all explicit prompt requirements are addressed.

| Element | Points | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Covers all 3 days distinctly | 5 | Not just a generic "SF highlights" list |
| Addresses all 5 stated interests (food, walking, bookstores, scenic views, low-key nightlife) | 10 | 2 pts each; deduct if any interest is ignored or only token-mentioned |
| Includes neighborhood structure | 3 | Not just point-to-point stops with no area grouping |
| Includes transit notes | 3 | Practical guidance, not just "take an Uber" |
| Includes rainy-day alternatives | 4 | Must be genuine alternatives (indoor), not just "bring an umbrella" |

---

## 3. Assumptions (15 points)
Assesses how well the response handles ambiguity in the prompt.

| Score | Criteria |
|---|---|
| 5 | Reasonably infers traveler context (e.g., assumes moderate fitness for walking, doesn't assume car rental, assumes budget-neutral unless stated) |
| 5 | Flags or states assumptions explicitly where the prompt is silent (e.g., "assuming arrival by midday on Day 1") rather than silently guessing |
| 5 | Doesn't over-assume unstated preferences (e.g., doesn't assume a specific cuisine type, doesn't assume solo vs. group travel without noting it) |

**Bonus consideration:** Does it ask clarifying questions or note where more info would sharpen the plan (dates/season affecting weather, dietary restrictions)?

---

## 4. Usability (20 points)
Assesses whether the plan is actually actionable and pleasant to follow.

| Score | Criteria |
|---|---|
| 5 | Pacing is genuinely relaxed — explicitly avoids over-scheduling, includes buffer/free time as requested |
| 5 | Format is scannable (day-by-day structure, clear time blocks or sequence, not a wall of prose) |
| 5 | Logical geographic flow (groups nearby activities, doesn't zigzag across the city unnecessarily) |
| 5 | Practical details included (e.g., meal timing makes sense, walking segments are sequenced with rest/food breaks) |

---

## 5. Risk (15 points)
Assesses safety, robustness, and responsible handling of edge cases.

| Score | Criteria |
|---|---|
| 4 | Rainy-day alternatives are realistic and sufficient (not an afterthought — genuinely substitutable) |
| 4 | Notes any relevant safety/practical caveats (e.g., specific areas with known safety considerations at night, if relevant to nightlife) |
| 4 | Avoids overconfident specificity that could mislead (e.g., doesn't state exact current hours/prices as fact if these change frequently — appropriately hedges) |
| 3 | Doesn't recommend anything requiring bookings/reservations without flagging the need to book ahead |

---

## Scoring Summary

| Category | Points |
|---|---|
| Correctness | /25 |
| Completeness | /25 |
| Assumptions | /15 |
| Usability | /20 |
| Risk | /15 |
| **Total** | **/100** |

**Suggested thresholds:** 90+ excellent/ready to use; 75–89 good with minor edits; 60–74 usable but needs revision; <60 significant rework needed.

output_text:
Below is a realistic 6-hour weekend plan to get you productive enough to analyze product usage data with SQL. The goal is not to “master SQL,” but to be able to answer common product questions like:

- How many users used a feature?
- Which users are active?
- What are the top events?
- How does usage differ by plan, country, or signup cohort?
- Where do users drop off in a funnel?
- How do I join usage data to user/account data?

---

## Before you start: pick your SQL environment

Ideally, use your work analytics tool or data warehouse, such as:

- BigQuery
- Snowflake
- Redshift
- Databricks
- Mode
- Looker/Looker Studio SQL Runner
- Metabase
- Superset

If you do not have access yet, use a free online SQL playground or install DuckDB/SQLite locally. But since your goal is work usage data, practicing in your company’s actual environment is best.

For this plan, assume your product data has tables like:

```sql
users
- user_id
- signup_date
- country
- plan
- acquisition_channel

events
- event_id
- user_id
- event_time
- event_name
- feature_name
- session_id

accounts
- account_id
- account_name
- plan
- created_at

account_users
- account_id
- user_id
- role
```

Your actual table names may differ, but most product analytics schemas look roughly like this.

---

# 6-Hour SQL Study Plan

## Hour 1: SQL basics — reading data from one table

### Goal

Learn how to inspect tables and pull specific rows/columns.

### Concepts to learn

- `SELECT`
- `FROM`
- `LIMIT`
- `WHERE`
- `ORDER BY`
- Basic comparison operators: `=`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`
- Filtering text values
- Filtering dates
- `AND`, `OR`, `IN`

### Spreadsheet analogy

Think of a SQL table like a spreadsheet tab.

- `SELECT` chooses columns.
- `WHERE` filters rows.
- `ORDER BY` sorts rows.
- `LIMIT` shows only the first N rows.

### Practice queries

Start by previewing tables:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM users
LIMIT 20;
```

```sql
SELECT *
FROM events
LIMIT 20;
```

Select specific columns:

```sql
SELECT
  user_id,
  signup_date,
  country,
  plan
FROM users
LIMIT 50;
```

Find users on a specific plan:

```sql
SELECT
  user_id,
  signup_date,
  country,
  plan
FROM users
WHERE plan = 'Pro'
LIMIT 50;
```

Find recent events:

```sql
SELECT
  user_id,
  event_time,
  event_name,
  feature_name
FROM events
WHERE event_time >= '2024-01-01'
ORDER BY event_time DESC
LIMIT 100;
```

Find specific event types:

```sql
SELECT
  user_id,
  event_time,
  event_name
FROM events
WHERE event_name IN ('signup_completed', 'project_created', 'invite_sent')
ORDER BY event_time DESC
LIMIT 100;
```

### Exercises

Write SQL queries to answer:

1. Show 25 users from the `users` table.
2. Show only `user_id`, `signup_date`, and `plan`.
3. Find all users from a specific country.
4. Find all events from the last 30 days.
5. Find all events where `event_name = 'login'`.
6. Find the 100 most recent events.

### Checkpoint

You are ready to move on if you can confidently answer:

- How do I preview a table?
- How do I select only the columns I need?
- How do I filter rows?
- How do I sort results?
- How do I limit the number of rows returned?

---

## Hour 2: Counting, grouping, and summarizing product usage

### Goal

Learn to answer basic product analytics questions such as “how many users did X?”

### Concepts to learn

- `COUNT(*)`
- `COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)`
- `GROUP BY`
- `ORDER BY`
- `MIN`, `MAX`, `SUM`, `AVG`
- Difference between event count and user count

### Important product analytics distinction

This query counts events:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_events
FROM events;
```

This query counts unique users:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS unique_users
FROM events;
```

Those are very different.

One user can generate many events.

### Practice queries

Count total events:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_events
FROM events;
```

Count unique active users:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS active_users
FROM events;
```

Count events by event type:

```sql
SELECT
  event_name,
  COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM events
GROUP BY event_name
ORDER BY event_count DESC;
```

Count unique users by event type:

```sql
SELECT
  event_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users
FROM events
GROUP BY event_name
ORDER BY users DESC;
```

Count signups by plan:

```sql
SELECT
  plan,
  COUNT(*) AS users
FROM users
GROUP BY plan
ORDER BY users DESC;
```

Find first and last event time:

```sql
SELECT
  MIN(event_time) AS first_event_time,
  MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM events;
```

### Exercises

Write queries to answer:

1. How many total users are in the `users` table?
2. How many total events are in the `events` table?
3. How many unique users have generated at least one event?
4. What are the top 10 most common event names?
5. Which features have the most unique users?
6. How many users are on each plan?
7. Which country has the most users?

### Checkpoint

You are ready to move on if you understand:

- `COUNT(*)` counts rows.
- `COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)` counts unique users.
- `GROUP BY` creates one row per group.
- Aggregations like `COUNT`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `AVG`, and `SUM` summarize data.

---

## Hour 3: Dates, time periods, and active users

### Goal

Learn how to analyze product usage over time.

### Concepts to learn

- Filtering by date
- Grouping by day/week/month
- Daily active users, weekly active users, monthly active users
- Time windows such as “last 7 days” or “last 30 days”

Date syntax varies by SQL system. The exact functions may be different in your workplace database.

Common examples:

In BigQuery:

```sql
DATE(event_time)
DATE_TRUNC(DATE(event_time), WEEK)
DATE_TRUNC(DATE(event_time), MONTH)
```

In Snowflake/Postgres-style SQL:

```sql
DATE_TRUNC('day', event_time)
DATE_TRUNC('week', event_time)
DATE_TRUNC('month', event_time)
```

### Practice queries

Daily active users:

```sql
SELECT
  DATE(event_time) AS event_date,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS daily_active_users
FROM events
GROUP BY DATE(event_time)
ORDER BY event_date;
```

Weekly active users, Postgres/Snowflake style:

```sql
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('week', event_time) AS week_start,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS weekly_active_users
FROM events
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('week', event_time)
ORDER BY week_start;
```

Monthly active users:

```sql
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', event_time) AS month_start,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS monthly_active_users
FROM events
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', event_time)
ORDER BY month_start;
```

Users who used a feature in the last 30 days:

```sql
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users_last_30_days
FROM events
WHERE event_time >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
  AND feature_name = 'Reporting';
```

Events per day:

```sql
SELECT
  DATE(event_time) AS event_date,
  COUNT(*) AS events
FROM events
GROUP BY DATE(event_time)
ORDER BY event_date;
```

### Exercises

Write queries to answer:

1. How many events happened each day?
2. How many unique users were active each day?
3. How many unique users were active each week?
4. How many users used a specific feature in the last 7 days?
5. What was the busiest day in the last 30 days?
6. How has usage of a key event changed by month?

### Checkpoint

You should be able to answer:

- What is DAU?
- How is DAU different from total daily events?
- How do I group product usage by day, week, or month?
- How do I filter to the last 7, 30, or 90 days?

---

## Hour 4: Joining tables

### Goal

Learn how to combine product usage data with user, account, or subscription data.

### Concepts to learn

- `JOIN`
- `LEFT JOIN`
- Join keys
- Table aliases
- Avoiding duplicate rows
- Joining events to users

### Spreadsheet analogy

A SQL `JOIN` is similar to a lookup, like `VLOOKUP` or `XLOOKUP`, except more powerful.

Example:

- `events` has `user_id`
- `users` has `user_id`
- Join them to analyze events by plan, country, signup channel, etc.

### Practice queries

Join events to users:

```sql
SELECT
  e.event_time,
  e.event_name,
  e.feature_name,
  e.user_id,
  u.plan,
  u.country,
  u.signup_date
FROM events e
JOIN users u
  ON e.user_id = u.user_id
LIMIT 100;
```

Count active users by plan:

```sql
SELECT
  u.plan,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e.user_id) AS active_users
FROM events e
JOIN users u
  ON e.user_id = u.user_id
GROUP BY u.plan
ORDER BY active_users DESC;
```

Count feature users by plan:

```sql
SELECT
  u.plan,
  e.feature_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e.user_id) AS users
FROM events e
JOIN users u
  ON e.user_id = u.user_id
WHERE e.event_time >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY
  u.plan,
  e.feature_name
ORDER BY users DESC;
```

Count users with and without activity using `LEFT JOIN`:

```sql
SELECT
  u.plan,
  COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) AS total_users,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e.user_id) AS active_users
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN events e
  ON u.user_id = e.user_id
  AND e.event_time >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY u.plan
ORDER BY total_users DESC;
```

Notice the date filter is inside the join condition. This preserves users with no events.

### Exercises

Write queries to answer:

1. How many active users are there by plan?
2. How many active users are there by country?
3. Which acquisition channel produces the most active users?
4. What percentage of users on each plan were active in the last 30 days?
5. Which features are most used by Pro users?
6. Which users signed up but have never generated an event?

### Checkpoint

You are ready to move on if you can explain:

- What column links two tables?
- When should I use `JOIN` versus `LEFT JOIN`?
- Why can joins accidentally create duplicate rows?
- How do I count users after joining without overcounting?

Key habit: when analyzing users, often use:

```sql
COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)
```

not:

```sql
COUNT(*)
```

---

## Hour 5: Practical product analysis patterns

### Goal

Use SQL for realistic product analytics tasks.

### Concepts to learn

- `CASE WHEN`
- Calculated columns
- Common Table Expressions, or CTEs, using `WITH`
- Funnels
- Simple retention/cohort thinking
- Activation analysis

---

## 5A. CASE statements

`CASE` is like an `IF` formula in spreadsheets.

Example: classify users by plan type.

```sql
SELECT
  user_id,
  plan,
  CASE
    WHEN plan IN ('Pro', 'Enterprise') THEN 'paid'
    ELSE 'free'
  END AS plan_type
FROM users;
```

Group users by signup age:

```sql
SELECT
  user_id,
  signup_date,
  CASE
    WHEN signup_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days' THEN 'new'
    WHEN signup_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' THEN 'recent'
    ELSE 'older'
  END AS signup_age_group
FROM users;
```

---

## 5B. CTEs

A CTE lets you break a query into steps.

Basic structure:

```sql
WITH active_users AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id
  FROM events
  WHERE event_time >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
)

SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS active_user_count
FROM active_users;
```

Think of a CTE as a temporary named table inside your query.

---

## 5C. Activation analysis

Example question:

“What percentage of new users created a project within 7 days of signup?”

```sql
WITH new_users AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    signup_date
  FROM users
  WHERE signup_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days'
),

activated_users AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
    u.user_id
  FROM new_users u
  JOIN events e
    ON u.user_id = e.user_id
   AND e.event_name = 'project_created'
   AND e.event_time >= u.signup_date
   AND e.event_time < u.signup_date + INTERVAL '7 days'
)

SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT n.user_id) AS new_users,
  COUNT(DISTINCT a.user_id) AS activated_users,
  1.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT a.user_id) / COUNT(DISTINCT n.user_id) AS activation_rate
FROM new_users n
LEFT JOIN activated_users a
  ON n.user_id = a.user_id;
```

---

## 5D. Simple funnel analysis

Example funnel:

1. Signed up
2. Created project
3. Invited teammate
4. Used key feature

```sql
WITH user_steps AS (
  SELECT
    u.user_id,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_name = 'project_created' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS created_project,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_name = 'invite_sent' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS invited_teammate,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_name = 'key_feature_used' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS used_key_feature
  FROM users u
  LEFT JOIN events e
    ON u.user_id = e.user_id
  GROUP BY u.user_id
)

SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS signed_up_users,
  SUM(created_project) AS created_project_users,
  SUM(invited_teammate) AS invited_teammate_users,
  SUM(used_key_feature) AS used_key_feature_users
FROM user_steps;
```

---

## Exercises

Write queries to answer:

1. Create a paid/free label using `CASE`.
2. Count active users by paid/free label.
3. Find users who signed up in the last 30 days and used a specific feature.
4. Calculate the percentage of new users who performed an activation event.
5. Build a simple 3-step funnel.
6. Compare activation rate by plan or acquisition channel.

### Checkpoint

You should now be able to:

- Create derived categories with `CASE`.
- Break complex queries into steps with `WITH`.
- Build a basic funnel.
- Calculate simple percentages.
- Analyze usage by user attributes like plan, country, or channel.

---

## Hour 6: Capstone project using your work data

### Goal

Spend the final hour answering a real product usage question from work.

Choose one realistic business question, such as:

1. “How many users used Feature X in the last 30 days?”
2. “Which plan has the highest usage of Feature X?”
3. “What percentage of new users activate within 7 days?”
4. “What are the top 10 events by unique users?”
5. “Which accounts are most active?”
6. “Where do users drop off in onboarding?”

---

# Capstone Option A: Feature usage report

### Question

“How is Feature X being used?”

### Queries to write

Total users of Feature X:

```sql
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)
